one_line_takeaway: SkillTrojan shows that reusable third-party agent 'skills' are a new backdoor surface: encrypting a payload and splitting it across benign-looking skill invocations that reassemble only under a trigger yields up to 97.2% attack success at ~89% clean accuracy on GPT-5.2 while evading LLM safety scanners ~99% of the time.
key_finding: SkillTrojan achieves high ASR while largely preserving (and sometimes improving) clean-task ACC, outperforming prompt-/context-centric baselines especially on stronger models. On EHR SQL closed-weight GPT-5.2-1211-Global it reaches 97.2% ASR at 89.3% ACC (vs NON-ATTACK ACC 73.0%), while the strongest baseline there, AgentPoison, reaches only 58.3% ASR. The advantage grows with model capability because malicious behavior is routed through trusted tool execution rather than depending on the model 'agreeing' to a shallow injected instruction (which stronger models refuse). Static LLM-based skill scanners largely fail to detect the full attack: Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B and Llama-Guard-3-8B classify 98.2% and 99.4% of SkillTrojan skills as safe, versus flagging the naive unsegmented variant heavily (only 3.4% / 2.8% rated safe). Ablations show N=3 is optimal (unimodal ASR curve), ASR/ACC are stable across poisoning ratio rho in {0.1,0.2,0.5}, and swapping XOR+Base64 for a hybrid crypto leaves ACC/ASR essentially unchanged (differ by 0.1/0.3 pts) while dropping a base64-heuristic detector's flag rate from 78% to 21%.
core_contribution: SkillTrojan is presented as the first backdoor attack that targets the *skill abstraction layer* of LLM agent systems rather than model parameters, training data, prompts, or tool/memory interfaces. A skill is modeled as a distributable package s=(m,A): a natural-language specification (SKILL.md) plus executable scripts/resources. The attacker publishes benign-looking, utility-improving skill packages that, once adopted from a marketplace/repo, hide an attacker-specified payload. The paper's own three claimed contributions are: (1) the skill-layer backdoor paradigm itself; (2) a general, extensible framework combining encrypted payload fragmentation, trigger-based activation, and automated synthesis of backdoored skills from arbitrary templates; and (3) an empirical evaluation across open- and closed-weight models plus release of SkillTrojanX, a dataset of 3,000+ curated backdoored skills. Distinct from cited agent-backdoor work (AgentPoison, BadChain, DemonAgent, memory/prompt poisoning), which the paper positions as operating on transient interaction channels within a single episode, SkillTrojan's payload persists in the installed skill package and is reconstructed through *normal skill composition* as an execution side effect.
threat_model: A skill-based agent dynamically loads third-party skills from public repositories/marketplaces. The base LLM and its training data are assumed TRUSTED and unmodified; the added risk comes solely from importing external skill packages that bundle instructions with executable code. The attacker's capability is to publish/distribute skills that look benign and improve task performance, inducing developer adoption; formally the attacker replaces a subset of skills with backdoored packages, specifying an arbitrary payload program P and a trigger predicate Trig_tau parameterized by a trigger string/pattern. AFTER installation the attacker has NO host access, OS privileges, or model internals; interaction is only through user queries. Objective is twofold: stealth on clean (non-triggered) inputs preserving nominal behavior, and reliable activation on triggered inputs causing P to run. Persistence arises only from the installed/reused skill package; fragment-reconstruction artifacts are ephemeral and run-scoped (written to tool return values / trajectory logs / a run-local scratchpad) and cleaned up after each run. Explicitly OUT of scope: cross-run state or long-term storage, base-model compromise, remote-service or OS compromise; sandboxing and output validation are assumed unavailable unless the deployment enables them.
limitations: Reported/inherent limits: (1) the attack requires the attacker to get backdoored skills adopted by developers -- social/distribution assumption outside the technical evaluation. (2) It explicitly excludes cross-run persistence and assumes no sandboxing or output validation by default; deployments enabling those (permissioned resource access, execution-trajectory auditing) are noted as effective mitigations. (3) On the weaker open-weight Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B-Instruct SkillTrojan is comparatively weak (26.7% ASR, and its ACC 48.4% is BELOW NON-ATTACK 53.2%), showing the method is not uniformly dominant and depends on the model completing the intended tool chain. (4) Triggered runs incur a small but systematic increase in tool-call count (Figure 3), a residual signal a trajectory-aware monitor could exploit. (5) EHR SQL correctness relies on an LLM-as-a-judge yes/no protocol, a soft ground truth. (6) The cross-framework results (Table 8) use a separate benchmark and are explicitly not directly comparable to the EHR SQL numbers. As a dual-use attack paper, it provides no working defense, only defense directions (provenance, sandboxing, runtime trace monitoring).

datasets_used: EHR SQL (primary end-to-end text-to-SQL clinical benchmark, LLM-as-a-judge correctness)
SWE-Bench Verified (Appendix B, test-based patch criterion)
SkillTrojanX (released dataset: 1200 templates, 3000+ backdoored skills, 6 template categories, 4 payload families, 3 crypto variants, 3 N variants, 50 unique triggers)
A separate industry-oriented coding benchmark for cross-framework evaluation (Appendix G)
~500 sampled skills used for the LLM-scanner detection study
quantitative_results: GPT-5.2-1211-Global (EHR SQL): SkillTrojan 97.2% ASR / 89.3% ACC vs NON-ATTACK 0.0% ASR / 73.0% ACC; strongest baseline AgentPoison 58.3% ASR / 72.9% ACC
Qwen3-Max (EHR SQL): SkillTrojan 74.7% ASR / 86.6% ACC (NON-ATTACK ACC 82.7%)
Claude-Sonnet4.5 (EHR SQL): SkillTrojan 64.2% ASR / 90.7% ACC; Claude-Haiku-4.5 57.3% ASR / 82.7% ACC; GPT-4o-mini 54.3% ASR / 68.5% ACC
GLM-4.6 (open-weight EHR SQL): SkillTrojan 72.0% ASR / 81.3% ACC; GLM-4.7 62.1% ASR / 85.2% ACC; Qwen3-Coder 64.7% ASR / 76.3% ACC; Qwen3-VL-235B 26.7% ASR / 48.4% ACC (below NON-ATTACK 53.2%)

Scanner evasion (~500 skills): 98.2% (Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B) and 99.4% (Llama-Guard-3-8B) of SkillTrojan skills classified safe; Skills(Native) 100.0%/99.6% safe; SkillTrojan w/o enc./seg. only 3.4%/2.8% safe
Encryption ablation (GPT-4o-mini, N=3, rho=0.2): XOR+Base64 68.5 ACC / 54.3 ASR / 78% heuristic-flag vs Hybrid 68.4 ACC / 54.6 ASR / 21% heuristic-flag
SWE-Bench Verified: SkillTrojan ASR 92.8% (GPT-5.2, ACC 70.4%), 63.9% (Qwen3-Coder, ACC 65.7%), 66.4% (GLM-4.7, ACC 67.6%); GCG baseline only 10.3-26.1% ASR
Cross-framework ASR (Table 8): e.g., Qwen3 Coder Plus 98.53-100.00% across Claude-Code/IFlow/OpenHands/OpenClaw; Claude Sonnet 4.5 63.53-69.12%
Clean-utility (Table 7): SKILLS(NATIVE) gives highest clean ACC (e.g., GLM-4.7 88.7 vs SkillTrojan 85.2 vs NON-ATTACK 84.1), confirming ACC gains come from benign skill utility not the backdoor
Ablations: N=3 maximizes ASR (unimodal); ACC/ASR stable across poisoning ratio rho in {0.1,0.2,0.5}, ACC near 68.5% even at rho=0.5
baselines_compared: Non-attack (agent without the installed skill stack)
Skills (Native) (same benign skills, no backdoor logic)
